cards.text:
To define a class in C++:
{{c1::class}} ClassName
{
}{{c2::;}}}

cards.front: What is the main difference between a class and a struct in C++?
cards.back: Classes default to private member access, while structs default to public member access. Otherwise, they are functionally equivalent.

cards.text: By convention, class names should {{c1::start with a capital letter}}, and class types are typically named using {{c2::nouns}}.

cards.question: How do you instantiate a class object?
cards.answer:
Employee joe; // default initialization
Employee jane{}; // value initialization
cards.explanation: Class objects are instantiated just like fundamental types, using the class name as the type.

cards.text: Variables of class types are sometimes called {{c1::class objects}} or {{c2::class instances}}. The process of creating a variable is called {{c3::instantiation}}.

cards.back: . (dot operator) - used to access members of a class object

cards.text: To access a member of a class object: {{c1::objectName.memberName}}

cards.front: What are the two types of members a class can have?

cards.question: What does this code print?
cards.explanation: The member variable value is initialized with default member initializer {42}. The object d gets this value upon instantiation.

cards.concept: When to use struct vs class
cards.explanation: Use struct for passive data structures with all public members (aggregates). Use class for objects that need to maintain invariants, have behaviors, or need private members for data hiding.
examples: struct Point { int x; int y; };  // passive data
class BankAccount { private: double balance; };  // data hiding

cards.front: Where should class type definitions typically be placed in a program?
cards.back: In a header file, so the type definition can be included and used in multiple code files without violating the one-definition rule (ODR).

cards.text: Unlike functions, class type definitions {{c1::must include a semicolon}} after the closing brace.
